abstract: Quality higher education serves as a fundamental pillar for a nation’s socio-economic advancement, with service quality acting as a primary metric for institutional success. This study examines the persistent challenges facing the Ethiopian higher education sector through a qualitative research lens. Data were gathered through a comprehensive analysis of 89 research papers, government policy documents, peer-reviewed articles, and doctoral dissertations, supplemented by semi-structured interviews and focus group discussions. The findings indicate that despite the implementation of various quality assurance frameworks, these initiatives have had a negligible impact on overall standards. Critical systemic deficiencies remain, including a shortage of experienced academic staff, inadequate ICT infrastructure, and subpar library and laboratory facilities. Furthermore, students continue to face poor living conditions and overcrowded classrooms. The study concludes that the majority of Ethiopian higher education institutions struggle with substandard service delivery, hindering their academic mission. Based on these insights, the paper offers strategic recommendations to address these infrastructure and personnel gaps.
